is an illustration of a single principle or strategy. So it's narrowed down to like one big idea, which is designed to make it like easy to deal with, to grasp. It's actually usually proof of a principle or strategy that we teach all the time. I'm not breaking any new ground there. The phenomenon page added recently is, of course, really a big promotional device. Its intent is to reinforce that we are the key to speed. The resources section, I try and give them free things that they can get or goose chases they can go on, because people like goose chases. That's activity masquerading as accomplishment. So if they get sent to this website and they get to get some free shit, or they get to go over here and get this, or somebody will send them that, that's like the same as going out and making $100,000 or doing something in their business. It has the same emotional impact. So they feel like they did something. Obviously, the resources section promotes us. The resources section gives us a place to promote others for whatever purposes, from generosity to it's really somebody we think they ought to know about, to it's somebody we really think they ought to know about, but that we're getting reciprocal support from. Almost none of it is direct money, but a lot of it is quid pro quo. And a spotlight on ascension. What did you miss if you weren't this kind of member last month? What's coming up if you're this kind of member next month? The Renegade Millionaire Backpage is, of course, all philosophical, and the final thoughts tend to be emotional bonding stuff. Then here's all the sort of specific content elements, starting with KK, I'm on the third page of the checklist, if you're lost. So here's all the different kind of member recognition I try and do. I'm on this page right here. Are you squinting for a reason? Yeah, okay. So member recognition has proof of value. Member recognition has self-aggrandizement. So the member who in their report of their success is tearfully or emotionally talking about the change in their life. And sometimes I'll use humor stuff, where somebody will talk about how, I'm the last person they pay attention to when they go to sleep or that kind of stuff. So self-aggrandizement stuff. Member recognition that counters specific objections, skepticism, or excuses. So you will see, for example, I will often point out, like here's a member who took massive action. They took this idea and they used it 16 ways. Or here's this member who went and did this thing right out of last month's issue, and in the first week, this, that, or the other thing happened. Well, what am I trying to reinforce, right? Member recognition is promotion. Somehow recognizing the member gets me an opportunity to talk about Go Plus, or Peak Performers, or a particular product, or an event, or something. Member recognition to encourage involvement, which generally recognition encourages involvement. But like the recognition of they sent me a book, they sent me an article, that kind of stuff encourages more people to send me more books and more articles, which by job easier. And again, is activity versus accomplishment. See, they think they accomplished something when they did that. So the act of finding something that they think I ought to see in sending it to me, for many of them, is the same as going out and making a sale. Now, it shouldn't be, but that's neither here nor there, it is. And so they feel like they did something. Member recognition to highlight ascension. Specific promotion in the copy, or as footnotes to content. Recently, you'll see we added a marketing 101 thing. That's sort of part of a response to early retention issues. We're doing a monstrous amount of things to retool the November experience. And so that's sort of a nod at that. Is a recognition that we got a lot of new members. And they get, and you mostly have the same problem. You know, they get dropped into this environment where they are a stranger in a strange land. And they have to be because to be intimate, we have to write to our members as if we have a long standing intimate relationship. But now this new person has shown up and they don't know the language and they don't know what the hell's going on. And they feel uncomfortable and they, you know, it's like, to me, it's like, you know, showing up at a cocktail party and nobody told you it was tuxedos. And everybody's in a tux except you and you're in a brown suit, you know. If you can leave, you leave, right? It's the Southwest Airlines commercials of, you know, I want to get out of here and I want to get out of here right now. Well, that's the reaction somebody has if we drop them into a place where they feel really, really, really uncomfortable. And so these early retention problems have a lot to do with that. So the marketing 101 thing is kind of an odd to that. Demonstrations of wizardry and pronouncements of wizardry. They are two different things, of course, but they are linked. So demonstration of wizardry now is actually showing off is, oh, here's how I take this bad headline and turn it into a really cool headline. See that? That's cool. Here's how I rewrite this guy's ad. So here's his bad ad. Here's how much better it is. Isn't that cool? So demonstrations of wizardry, pronouncements of wizardry are much more common because they don't require me to do the work of rewriting the guy's ad. So that's just telling people you're a wizard. Statements of value, keeping pointing out. You will, I'll bet you if you went through all the newsletters in a year and you count and you stick counted it, I'll bet you there's a thousand times where in one way, shape, or form, I say now this single item pays for your subscription for the whole year. This alone was worth the price of admission. This one next thing is a million dollar idea. See, that's stating value. That's driving home value. And it's sort of like Trump in grain now doing the biggest, greatest, grandest. I do that automatically. I do it verbally. I do it in print. I don't think about it anymore, but I do it and I do it a lot. If you don't tell them it's valuable, they may not know it's valuable. I want to be like Dan's stuff. And that falls into two categories. That falls into gee, I wish I had Dan's balls. Boy, I wish I would say that to somebody. I wish I would not negotiate my fee, Darren, like we were talking about it. Boy, I wish that. So it's attitudinal stuff and then it's lifestyle stuff and what the lifestyle stuff represents. Warning against bad behavior. Real inspiration, showing them exciting examples of what's possible, which like the phenomenon page is a place now where we do that. Guilt, shame and adequacy, challenging statements, challenging them to measure up. And a little bit of political social comment. The objectives pretty much in order. Our connection, reconnection with the place. You're in the right place, you just crossed into a new world, the members connection with me. Interest, entertainment, reinforcement, promotion, variety, trying to give something to everybody because we got a mixed dog. See some of you have a much easier job in this category than we do. But if you think about it, even in a single breed of dog, you got old dogs, young dogs, dumb dogs, smart dogs, lazy dogs, ambitious dogs. So you got a lot of different, you got variety even within a non-veried environment if you will. Take away value, involvement, which is the hardest thing to pull off and it's always the smallest percentage of people who do it and just an overall satisfying experience. So then when they get done, they have an open to the envelope and played with the stuff and looked at it and everything, they feel pretty good about what they've done this month. You will notice the take away value is fairly low on the list. There are other things that are far more important. The feelings we're after on the last page are also listed. And so I want them to feel they're in a continuing relationship and in a conversation. So I try very hard to write as if, even though it's all a monologue of course, it needs to feel like we're in this ongoing conversation. One of the ways you know you get that right is if you're at an event or you're on a teleconference or whatever and they now start with what was in a newsletter. And so the language people will often use with me is when we were talking about X last month, not when you wrote about X last month, the language they'll use with me is when we were talking about X last month. But see in their head, there's a dialogue going on that I like them, care about them and understand them, that they're part of something, that there's great value here and if they're not getting it, it's their fault. So that's real important. And of course you get that by showing them the examples that tell them the stories and all of that. So they gotta know if they're not getting it, it's their fault and the solution to that is to hang around longer and work harder.
